headline_summary: Strait of Hormuz shipping disruption persists, with severe traffic reduction/backlogs and reports of limited, coordinated transits (Reuters, Mar 29; Reuters, Mar 5)
Oil extends gains on conflict and chokepoint risk; market pricing reflects elevated Middle East supply/shipping risk premia (Reuters, Mar 29-30)
Pakistan hosts Turkey/Egypt/Saudi talks on proposals to reopen Strait of Hormuz and end Iran war (Reuters, Mar 29)
Iranian strikes/drones reported to damage industrial and infrastructure targets in the Gulf, including EGA’s Al Taweelah site and Kuwait airport radar (Reuters, Mar 28)
War-risk insurance premiums and shipping costs surge as Gulf conflict widens and Hormuz traffic is paralyzed (Reuters, Mar 6)
China-linked shipping signals stress: COSCO suspends bookings; two Chinese COSCO container ships abort attempted Hormuz exit (Reuters, Mar 4; Reuters, Mar 27)
Qatar declares force majeure on LNG exports amid Gulf conflict, highlighting global gas supply chain risk (Reuters, Mar 4)
Iran declares Strait of Hormuz closed and threatens ships attempting to transit (Reuters, Mar 2)
US banks placed on heightened alert for potential Iran-linked cyberattacks as war escalates (Reuters, Mar 3)

Sanctions/tactical policy actions continue alongside kinetic escalation: US announces additional Iran-linked sanctions; earlier 30-day waiver for Iranian-origin oil loaded at sea (Reuters, Mar 3; Reuters, Mar 20)
risk_drivers: Strait of Hormuz closure threats and sharply reduced vessel traffic
Direct attacks/incidents affecting commercial shipping and Gulf energy/logistics infrastructure
LNG supply disruption risk (Qatar force majeure) and spillover to global gas markets
Rising war-risk insurance and freight costs amplifying delivered energy prices
Escalation management uncertainty despite ongoing diplomacy (Hormuz reopening proposals)
Second-order cyber risk to financial sector amid conflict escalation

Energy: Higher crude risk premium while Hormuz traffic remains constrained; tail risk increases when closure threats are reiterated alongside tanker incidents.
LNG upside risk if Qatar’s force majeure and Gulf logistics constraints persist; knock-on effects to Asian and European spot markets.
Refining and petrochemical margins may widen on feedstock volatility; Middle East export schedules become less reliable.
FX: Oil-importer EM FX faces pressure on higher energy import bills; safe-haven demand can support USD in risk-off episodes.
Gulf FX pegs remain supported by policy frameworks but sensitivity rises if export receipts are interrupted for longer.
Rates: Energy-driven inflation risk can keep rate-cut expectations constrained and reprice breakevens.
Credit spreads can widen for airlines/shipping/energy-intensive sectors as insurance and logistics costs surge.
Equities: Risk-off rotations favor energy/defense; pressure on airlines, shipping, and industrials tied to Middle East supply chains.
Gulf equities can lag on geopolitical discount; global cyclicals sensitive to freight/insurance cost shock.

sources.title: Oil prices jump after Yemeni Houthis attack Israel, widening Iran conflict
Two India-bound LPG tankers clear Strait of Hormuz, government says
Pakistan hosts regional powers for Iran talks, with focus on Hormuz proposals
Emirates Global Aluminium reports 'significant damage' from Iranian strikes
Kuwait airport radar system significantly damaged after drone attack, authorities say
Iran declares Strait of Hormuz closed and threatens ships attempting to transit
Qatar shuts gas liquefaction and declares force majeure on LNG exports
Maritime war-risk insurance premiums surge as Gulf conflict widens and Hormuz traffic is paralyzed
US banks on alert for potential Iran-linked cyberattacks as war escalates
US announces additional Iran-linked sanctions amid conflict

events.title: Strait of Hormuz traffic disruption persists; limited coordinated transits reported
events.implication: Persistent disruption raises delivered crude/LNG risk premia and increases odds of supply chain rerouting and higher freight/insurance costs.

events.title: Houthis attack Israel; oil jumps on widening Iran conflict
events.implication: Cross-theater escalation amplifies risk-off and energy up-risk, raising tail risks for Red Sea and Gulf shipping and regional retaliation cycles.

events.title: Pakistan hosts Turkey/Egypt/Saudi talks focused on proposals to reopen Hormuz and end Iran war
events.implication: If credible, diplomacy could reduce chokepoint risk premia; markets will watch for verifiable de-escalation steps and shipping normalization.

events.title: Iranian strikes cause significant damage at Emirates Global Aluminium Al Taweelah site
events.implication: Direct hits on Gulf industrial infrastructure increase escalation risk and lift insurance/freight costs; potential knock-on to metals supply chains.

events.title: Drone attack significantly damages Kuwait International Airport radar system
events.implication: Infrastructure damage heightens regional risk premium and could disrupt aviation/logistics; adds to market perception of widening target set.

events.title: COSCO suspends bookings amid Gulf conflict; China-linked shipping signals stress
events.implication: Container capacity withdrawals can tighten global logistics and raise freight rates; reinforces chokepoint risk affecting delivered energy and goods prices.

events.title: Qatar declares force majeure on LNG exports after shutting gas liquefaction
events.implication: Potential LNG supply interruption drives global gas price upside risk and forces buyers into spot markets; can intensify broader risk-off.

events.title: US announces additional Iran-linked sanctions amid conflict escalation
events.implication: Sanctions can tighten effective supply and increase compliance costs; may also provoke retaliation impacting shipping/security.

events.title: US banks placed on heightened alert for potential Iran-linked cyberattacks
events.implication: Cyber risk can amplify financial volatility and risk premia beyond energy; increases tail risk of operational disruptions.

events.title: Iran declares Strait of Hormuz closed and threatens ships attempting to transit
events.implication: Closure threats represent a high-severity tail risk for global oil/LNG flows; even partial enforcement lifts war-risk and freight costs materially.

events.title: War-risk insurance premiums surge as Gulf conflict widens and Hormuz traffic is paralyzed
events.implication: Higher insurance premia and freight tighten effective supply and raise delivered prices; negative for risk assets and supportive of energy.

links.why: Gas tightness can lift oil-linked demand and broader energy complex risk premia.

links.why: Potential tightening in energy supply/price increases can worsen terms of trade for oil importers.

links.why: Diplomatic proposals target restoration of shipping flows, though outcomes uncertain.
